questions.explanation: In mathematics, “undefined” means the value is not well-defined (e.g., 1/0). Multiplying by 0 does not rescue the expression because the operation still depends on an undefined operand. Therefore 0 × undefined is undefined. In ML practice, this often manifests as NaN propagation through feature engineering unless explicitly handled.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes *classic* Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) when training base learners?
options: A. It bootstraps instances and also selects a random subset of features at each tree split.
B. It bootstraps instances but uses all features (no feature subsetting) when training each base model.
C. It does not bootstrap instances; it trains each base model on the full dataset but subsets features.
D. It neither bootstraps instances nor subsets features; it is equivalent to training a single model.
questions.explanation: Classic bagging creates diversity by sampling training instances *with replacement* (bootstrapping) for each base learner. In its standard form, it does not perform random feature subsetting; each base learner can use the full feature set.

questions.question_text:
In Bayesian Optimization, if the objective function f(x) has no known closed-form expression, where do the initial function values y (e.g., y1, y2, ...) come from?

Choose the best answer.
options: They are computed by symbolically differentiating f(x) and evaluating the derivative at x.
They are obtained by running an experiment/measurement or a costly simulation at chosen inputs x.
They are sampled from the prior distribution of the surrogate model without evaluating the real function.
They are inferred solely from the acquisition function before any evaluations are performed.
questions.explanation: Bayesian Optimization assumes f is a black box: you cannot write f(x) in closed form, but you can query it. The initial y values are produced by actually evaluating the real process (e.g., training a model, running a simulator, performing a lab experiment) at selected inputs x, yielding observed outcomes y.

questions.question_text:
When we “draw a function” from a Gaussian Process (GP) in practice, what are we actually sampling?

Assume we choose inputs \(\mathbf{X} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}\).
options: A vector \(\mathbf{f} = (f(\mathbf{x}_1),\ldots,f(\mathbf{x}_N))^\top\) from a multivariate normal distribution with mean \(\mathbf{m}\) and covariance \(\mathbf{K}\).
An explicit closed-form function \(f(\mathbf{x})\) sampled directly over an uncountably infinite set of \(\mathbf{x}\) values.
Only independent Gaussian noise values \(\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\) without using the kernel matrix.
A single scalar \(f(\mathbf{x})\) at one point, which uniquely determines the entire GP sample path.
questions.explanation: A GP defines that for any finite set of inputs \(\mathbf{X}\), the corresponding function values \(\mathbf{f}\) are jointly Gaussian: \(\mathbf{f} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{m}, \mathbf{K})\) where \(\mathbf{m}_i=m(\mathbf{x}_i)\) and \(\mathbf{K}_{ij}=k(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)\). In practice, “drawing a function” means sampling this finite-dimensional vector; plotting/interpolating across a dense grid gives a sample path visualization.

questions.question_text: For evaluating a Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) regression model where large overestimation errors are especially costly, which metric is generally the best choice and why?
options: A. RMSE, because it penalizes large errors more heavily and is interpretable in dollars
B. MAE, because it penalizes large errors more heavily and is interpretable in dollars²
C. MSE, because it is the most interpretable in monetary units
D. R², because it directly measures average dollar error per customer
questions.explanation: RMSE squares errors before averaging and then takes a square root, which makes large errors contribute disproportionately to the final score. This is desirable for CLV when big misses (e.g., overestimating high-value customers) lead to costly decisions. Unlike MSE, RMSE is in the same units as the target (dollars), making it easier to interpret and communicate.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains a key advantage of the BG/NBD model over a general-purpose ML regression model when estimating CLV from repeat purchase data?
options: BG/NBD inherently accounts for censoring (customers still active) and models future transactions probabilistically using frequency, recency, and customer age (T).
BG/NBD can automatically use thousands of raw behavioral features without feature engineering and will always outperform tree-based models.
BG/NBD is non-parametric and therefore captures arbitrary non-linear interactions between all available customer covariates.
BG/NBD directly predicts monetary value per transaction without needing any additional modeling assumptions.
questions.explanation: BG/NBD is designed for repeat transaction processes and is parsimonious: it uses frequency, recency, and exposure time (T). A major advantage is that it naturally handles censoring—many customers are still “alive,” so their true lifetime is not fully observed. It also produces probabilistic outputs (expected transactions and uncertainty). The other options describe capabilities BG/NBD does not have (automatic high-dimensional feature usage, non-parametric arbitrary interactions, or direct monetary value prediction).

questions.question_text: In the BG/NBD model, which set of three customer-level summary inputs is typically used?
options: A. Frequency (repeat purchases), Recency (time between first and last purchase), T (time since first purchase)
B. Frequency (total purchases), Monetary value (average order value), T (time since last purchase)
C. Recency (time since last purchase), Monetary value (total spend), Churn label (0/1)
D. Frequency (repeat purchases), Recency (time since last purchase), T (time since last purchase)
questions.explanation: BG/NBD is commonly fit using three summary statistics per customer: frequency (repeat transactions beyond the first), recency (time between first and last transaction), and T (the customer's age in the observation window, i.e., time since first purchase). The other options either replace recency/T with 'time since last purchase' or introduce monetary/churn variables that are not part of the standard BG/NBD input trio.

questions.question_text:
BG/NBD models often perform poorly for customers with only one transaction. Which alternative approach is specifically designed to model the *time until a second purchase* (treating the second purchase as an event) and can naturally handle customers who have not yet repurchased?

A. K-Means clustering
B. Survival analysis (e.g., Kaplan–Meier, Cox model)
C. Ordinary least squares (OLS) linear regression on revenue
D. PCA followed by logistic regression
options: K-Means clustering
Survival analysis (e.g., Kaplan–Meier, Cox model)
Ordinary least squares (OLS) linear regression on revenue
PCA followed by logistic regression
questions.explanation: Survival analysis is explicitly built for time-to-event problems with censoring. For one-time buyers, the “event” can be defined as the second purchase, and customers who have not repurchased are right-censored (observed up to a time without the event). Kaplan–Meier estimates the survival function non-parametrically, and Cox models how features affect the hazard (repurchase rate). Clustering (A) can find similar customers but does not directly model time-to-event; OLS (C) targets continuous outcomes and ignores censoring/time; PCA+logistic (D) is not inherently time-to-event and typically models a binary outcome without time and censoring unless specially engineered.

questions.question_text: In the context of BG/NBD + Gamma-Gamma customer value modeling, what does the Series `expected_avg_profit` represent?
options: The predicted number of future transactions for each customer from the BG/NBD model
The estimated average profit per transaction for each customer predicted by the Gamma-Gamma model
The total expected profit for each customer over a future time horizon (i.e., CLV) computed directly by BG/NBD
The historical average profit per transaction computed from past data without modeling
questions.explanation: `expected_avg_profit` is described as a Series whose values are the Gamma-Gamma model’s estimate of each customer’s typical (future) profit per transaction, inferred from historical spending behavior. It is not the transaction count (BG/NBD), not total CLV by itself, and not a simple historical average without modeling.

questions.question_text: In which scenario is the BG/NBD model generally the *best fit* according to the provided content?
options: A typical monthly subscription where payments occur on a fixed schedule until explicit cancellation
A non-contractual setting with intermittent purchases where customers decide whether to buy each time
A setting where every customer renews deterministically every month with no late payments and no missed renewals
A setting where churn is fully observed and there is no need to model dropout probabilistically
questions.explanation: BG/NBD was originally designed for non-contractual contexts with intermittent transactions and unobserved dropout, where customers make a purchase decision each opportunity. Regular monthly subscriptions are more contractual and deterministic, so churn/survival models often fit better unless renewals are sufficiently uncertain.

questions.question_text:
In the BG/NBD model, which set of inputs is used to compute the probability that a customer is still active (P(alive))?

Assume customer-specific inputs are derived from purchase history and model parameters are learned from the population.
options: A. Customer-specific: x, tx, T; Model parameters: r, α, s, β
B. Customer-specific: r, α, s, β; Model parameters: x, tx, T
C. Customer-specific: x, monetary value, discount rate; Model parameters: k, λ, μ, σ
D. Customer-specific: tx only; Model parameters: r and α only
questions.explanation: BG/NBD uses customer history (frequency x, recency tx, and customer age/tenure T) together with global parameters learned from the dataset (r, α governing purchase process heterogeneity; s, β governing dropout heterogeneity) to compute P(alive). The other options either swap roles, introduce unrelated variables/parameters, or omit required inputs.

questions.question_text: In a BG/NBD + Gamma-Gamma CLV framework, which statement is most accurate for a customer who has made exactly one purchase (i.e., frequency = 0 in the typical BG/NBD definition)?
options: BG/NBD cannot produce any future transaction prediction when frequency = 0.
BG/NBD can still predict future transactions, but Gamma-Gamma is typically not used to estimate monetary value for this customer because there is no repeat-purchase monetary variability.
Gamma-Gamma will estimate monetary value accurately from a single transaction because it only needs one observation per customer.
If frequency = 0, the correct approach is to set predicted future transactions to zero and drop the customer from CLV calculations.
questions.explanation: BG/NBD uses frequency, recency, and customer “age” T to produce an expected number of future transactions even when frequency=0 (one observed purchase). However, the Gamma-Gamma model is generally fit/used for customers with repeat transactions (frequency>0) because it relies on information about a customer’s average transaction value, which is not well-identified from only one purchase (no within-customer variability).

questions.question_text: In the BG/NBD model, what does **frequency** mean for a given customer?
options: The total number of purchases the customer made (including the first purchase)
The number of repeat purchases (total purchases minus the first purchase)
The number of days between the first and last purchase
The number of purchases made in the first week after acquisition
questions.explanation: BG/NBD defines frequency as the count of **repeat** purchases only. The first purchase marks the start of the observed customer lifetime, so it is not counted toward frequency; thus frequency = (total purchases) − 1.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why the BG/NBD model is considered interpretable (rather than a black box)?
options: A. It provides SHAP values that rank the importance of each feature for every prediction.
B. It uses intuitive inputs (frequency, recency, T) and has explicit probabilistic assumptions that lead to closed-form predictions.
C. It is a deep neural network with attention weights that can be visualized to explain predictions.
D. It automatically discovers latent customer segments without any modeling assumptions.
questions.explanation: BG/NBD is interpretable because its inputs (frequency, recency, observation period T) are directly meaningful, it is built on explicit assumptions (Poisson transactions and Beta-Geometric dropout), and it produces predictions via deterministic closed-form formulas. It typically does not provide feature-importance artifacts like SHAP (A), and it is not a neural attention model (C) nor an assumption-free latent segmentation method (D).

questions.question_text: In Cohen’s Kappa for a binary classification task (Success/Failure), which expression correctly computes the expected agreement by chance \(P_e\) from the raters’ marginal label proportions?
options: B. \(P_e = (p_{yes,1} \times p_{yes,2}) + (p_{no,1} \times p_{no,2})\)
C. \(P_e = 1 - (p_{yes,1} \times p_{yes,2})\)
questions.explanation: Expected agreement by chance in Cohen’s Kappa assumes the raters choose labels independently according to their own marginal probabilities. The probability they both say “yes” is \(p_{yes,1}p_{yes,2}\), and the probability they both say “no” is \(p_{no,1}p_{no,2}\). Summing these gives \(P_e\). The other options either add probabilities incorrectly, ignore one class, or average unrelated quantities.

questions.question_text:
In a binary classification problem with imbalanced classes, you want to compute F1 separately for the positive and negative classes using scikit-learn. Which code correctly computes class-specific F1 for label 1 and label 0?

```python
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score

# y_true, y_pred are binary arrays with labels {0, 1}
```
options: A) f1_pos = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=1); f1_neg = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, pos_label=0)
B) f1_pos = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='macro'); f1_neg = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='weighted')
C) f1_pos = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, labels=[1]); f1_neg = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, labels=[0])
D) f1_pos = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='binary'); f1_neg = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='binary')
questions.explanation: In scikit-learn, for binary classification the per-class F1 can be computed by setting which label is treated as the “positive” class via `pos_label`. Using `pos_label=1` yields the usual positive-class F1, and `pos_label=0` yields the negative-class F1. Options B and D produce aggregate metrics (macro/weighted or default binary) rather than explicitly switching the positive label. Option C is not the standard way to select the positive class for `f1_score` in binary mode; `labels` is mainly used for multiclass/multilabel contexts and does not by itself define the positive label for binary averaging.

questions.question_text: In binary classification with predicted probabilities \(p_i = P(y_i=1)\), which expression correctly defines the **positive-class log-loss** (class-conditional log-loss for \(y=1\)) after clipping probabilities away from 0 and 1?
options: A. \(\displaystyle -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \log(p_i)\)
B. \(\displaystyle -\frac{1}{N_+}\sum_{i: y_i=1} \log(p_i)\)
C. \(\displaystyle -\frac{1}{N_+}\sum_{i: y_i=1} \log(1-p_i)\)
D. \(\displaystyle -\frac{1}{N_-}\sum_{i: y_i=0} \log(p_i)\)
questions.explanation: Positive-class log-loss averages the penalty for examples whose true label is 1, using the term from binary cross-entropy that applies when y=1: \(-\log(p_i)\). Therefore it is \(-\frac{1}{N_+}\sum_{i:y_i=1}\log(p_i)\). Options C and D use the wrong term or wrong subset, and A ignores conditioning on the positive class.

questions.question_text: In a binary classification setting, ROC AUC is often described as a probability involving scores for positives and negatives. Which statement best describes ROC AUC for the *original positive class*?
options: The probability that a randomly chosen negative instance has a higher score than a randomly chosen positive instance
The probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is scored higher than a randomly chosen negative instance
The fraction of predictions above 0.5 that are truly positive
The average of sensitivity and specificity across all thresholds
questions.explanation: ROC AUC can be interpreted as the probability that a randomly selected positive example receives a higher model score than a randomly selected negative example (assuming no ties, or with ties handled in the standard way). Options C and D describe other metrics (precision at a threshold; balanced accuracy-like idea), and A is the reversed inequality.

questions.question_text: In the standard (classic) BG/NBD model formulation, which inputs are used to fit the model for each customer?
options: A. Frequency, recency, and customer age (T)
B. Frequency, monetary value, and customer age (T)
C. Recency, customer age (T), and a vector of covariates (e.g., demographics)
D. Frequency, recency, and a vector of covariates (e.g., marketing touches)
questions.explanation: The classic BG/NBD model is designed to work with three summary statistics per customer: frequency (repeat purchases), recency (time of last purchase), and customer age/observation period length (T). It does not directly accept additional predictors/covariates in its standard formulation.

questions.question_text:
When choosing features for K-Means with the goal of forming balanced (equal-sized) clusters, which combination best follows the provided guidance on variance and correlation?

Assume the following: `opportunity_amount` has high variance and low correlation with others; `avg_num_days_spent_in_any_stage` has high variance and is fairly independent; `qualified_to_consensus_building_num_days` has high variance but moderate correlation with `sales_cycle_length_days`.
options: A. opportunity_amount, avg_num_days_spent_in_any_stage, qualified_to_consensus_building_num_days
B. has_follow_up_bigram, has_next_step_bigram, min_num_days_spent_in_any_stage
C. min_num_days_spent_in_any_stage, max_num_days_spent_in_any_stage, sales_cycle_length_days
D. avg_num_days_spent_in_any_stage, sales_cycle_length_days, qualified_to_consensus_building_num_days
questions.explanation: Option A matches the stated selection logic: prioritize higher-variance features that help separate points in space, and avoid redundant (highly correlated) predictors. Binary/low-variance features in B tend to contribute little to distance-based separation. C stacks likely correlated time-in-stage features (min/max) and includes lower-variance signals, increasing redundancy. D includes two features that are more likely to be correlated (`sales_cycle_length_days` with `qualified_to_consensus_building_num_days`), making it less diverse than A.

questions.question_text: Which algorithm is specifically designed to **enforce (approximately) equal-sized clusters** rather than only minimizing within-cluster variance like standard K-Means?
options: A. Standard K-Means
B. Balanced K-Means (constraint-based K-Means)
C. K-Medoids (PAM)
D. Agglomerative (hierarchical) clustering
questions.explanation: Balanced K-Means modifies the assignment step of K-Means by adding constraints (often via constrained assignment or integer programming) so each cluster has roughly the same number of points. Standard K-Means (A) optimizes variance only and can produce very imbalanced clusters. K-Medoids (C) can sometimes yield better balance in practice but does not explicitly enforce it. Agglomerative clustering (D) can be tuned (e.g., via thresholds/cut choices) but is not inherently an equal-size enforcement method.

questions.question_text:
In the context of combining an outlier detector (e.g., Isolation Forest) with an ExtraTrees classifier, which approach is best described as **training a second model on the predictions of the first-stage models** to learn how to combine them?

A. Weighted voting ensemble
B. Stacking
C. Conditional (rule-based) selection
D. Feature enrichment (using anomaly score as a feature)
options: Weighted voting ensemble
Stacking
Conditional (rule-based) selection
Feature enrichment (using anomaly score as a feature)
questions.explanation: Stacking trains a meta-model (second-stage model) using the outputs (predictions or scores) of base models as input features. Weighted voting combines predictions directly with fixed weights; conditional selection uses rules to choose which model to trust; feature enrichment augments the feature set with an anomaly score and trains ExtraTrees on that expanded feature space.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the key difference in how DAgger and SMILE generate trajectories during data collection?
options: DAgger samples between expert and learner at every timestep, while SMILE follows the learner for the entire episode.
DAgger follows the current learned policy for the entire episode, while SMILE stochastically switches between expert and learner at each timestep.
Both DAgger and SMILE always follow the expert policy during data collection; they differ only in how they train the classifier.
Both DAgger and SMILE always follow the learned policy during data collection; they differ only in whether they query the expert for labels.
questions.explanation: DAgger executes the learned policy π(i) throughout an episode (producing a trajectory from π(i)’s state distribution) and queries the expert for the correct action at each visited state. SMILE, in contrast, chooses at each timestep whether to act according to the expert (with probability p) or the learner (with probability 1−p), producing a blended (mixture) state distribution.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a key diagnostic implication of assuming your event data follow a (homogeneous) Poisson process?
options: A. Inter-arrival times are exponentially distributed and counts in fixed intervals are Poisson-distributed.
B. Inter-arrival times are normally distributed and counts in fixed intervals are binomially distributed.
C. Inter-arrival times are uniformly distributed and counts in fixed intervals are geometrically distributed.
D. Inter-arrival times are Poisson-distributed and counts in fixed intervals are exponentially distributed.
questions.explanation: A homogeneous Poisson process implies (1) independent, exponentially distributed inter-arrival times and (2) the number of events in any fixed-length interval follows a Poisson distribution. The other options swap distributions or pair incorrect ones (e.g., Poisson is for counts, not waiting times).

questions.question_text: Which formula correctly converts a covariance matrix element \(\Sigma_{ij}\) into a correlation matrix element \(R_{ij}\)?
options: A. \(R_{ij} = \Sigma_{ij} \cdot \sigma_i \sigma_j\)
B. \(R_{ij} = \frac{\Sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_i + \sigma_j}\)
C. \(R_{ij} = \frac{\Sigma_{ij}}{\sigma_i \sigma_j}\)
D. \(R_{ij} = \frac{\Sigma_{ij}}{\Sigma_{ii}\Sigma_{jj}}\)
questions.explanation: Correlation standardizes covariance by dividing by the product of the standard deviations: \(R_{ij} = \Sigma_{ij}/(\sigma_i\sigma_j)\) where \(\sigma_i=\sqrt{\Sigma_{ii}}\). This scaling ensures correlations are dimensionless and lie in \([-1,1]\). Options A and B use incorrect scaling, and D divides by variances rather than standard deviations (and is dimensionally incorrect).

questions.question_text: In binary classification, which statement best describes the relationship between (binary) cross-entropy loss and log loss?
options: They are the same objective (same formula) and are commonly used interchangeably in binary classification.
Cross-entropy is used only for multi-class classification, while log loss is used only for regression.
They differ by a constant factor of 1/2, so they produce different optimal models.
They are equivalent only when labels are in {-1, +1}, not when labels are in {0, 1}.
questions.explanation: For binary classification with labels y ∈ {0,1} and predicted probability ŷ = P(y=1), both binary cross-entropy and log loss use the same expression: −[y log(ŷ) + (1−y) log(1−ŷ)]. The difference is mainly naming: “cross-entropy” is common in information theory/deep learning, while “log loss” is common in ML libraries like scikit-learn.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the key difference between ROUGE-N (e.g., ROUGE-1/2) and ROUGE-L when evaluating a generated summary against a reference?
options: ROUGE-N uses the longest common subsequence (LCS), while ROUGE-L uses exact n-gram overlap.
ROUGE-N measures exact consecutive n-gram overlap, while ROUGE-L measures the longest sequence of words in the same order (not necessarily consecutive).
ROUGE-N evaluates semantic similarity using embeddings, while ROUGE-L evaluates syntactic similarity using parse trees.
ROUGE-N is precision-only, while ROUGE-L is recall-only.
questions.explanation: ROUGE-N (ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, etc.) is based on n-gram overlap, which requires consecutive matches of length N (e.g., bigrams must be adjacent). ROUGE-L is based on the Longest Common Subsequence, which preserves order but allows gaps, making it more sensitive to word order and sentence-level structure than strict n-gram matching.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the difference between a win/loss forecasting model and a customer lifetime value (CLV) model?
options: A win/loss model predicts a continuous revenue amount per customer, while a CLV model predicts whether a deal will close.
A win/loss model is a binary classification over opportunities/deals, while a CLV model predicts a continuous expected value (revenue/profit) over a customer’s lifecycle.
Both models are the same; they only differ in the algorithm (classification vs. regression).
A win/loss model is used for long-term retention planning, while a CLV model is used only for short-term pipeline forecasting.
questions.explanation: Win/loss forecasting targets a binary outcome (win=1/loss=0) at the opportunity level, typically to support short-term pipeline and sales prioritization. CLV targets a continuous monetary value (expected revenue/profit) at the customer level over a longer horizon, often incorporating retention/churn dynamics.

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the difference between the gradient and the Hessian of a scalar loss function \(\ell(\theta)\) with respect to parameters \(\theta\)?
options: A. The gradient is a matrix of second derivatives (curvature), while the Hessian is a vector of first derivatives (slope).
B. The gradient is a vector of first derivatives (slope), while the Hessian is a matrix of second derivatives (curvature).
C. Both the gradient and Hessian are vectors of first derivatives; they differ only in dimensionality.
D. The gradient is only defined for one-dimensional functions, while the Hessian is only defined for multi-dimensional functions.
questions.explanation: For a scalar loss \(\ell(\theta)\), the gradient \(\nabla \ell\) collects first partial derivatives (how the loss changes with small moves in each parameter direction), which corresponds to local slope/tilt. The Hessian \(H\) collects second partial derivatives \(H_{ij}=\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \theta_i \partial \theta_j}\), which describes curvature and how the slope changes as you move in parameter space.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the *per-iteration* computation and memory trade-off between Batch Gradient Descent (GD) and Stochastic Average Gradient (SAG)?
options: GD computes one sample gradient per step and stores an n×d table of past gradients.
SAG computes the full gradient per step and needs no extra memory beyond w.
GD computes the full gradient per step (touches all n samples) and needs little extra memory; SAG computes one fresh sample gradient per step but stores past gradients for all samples (≈ n×d).
Both GD and SAG compute the full gradient per step; SAG is faster only because it uses a larger learning rate.
questions.explanation: Batch GD forms the exact full gradient ∇f(w) = (1/n)∑ᵢ ∇ℓᵢ(w) each iteration, so it touches all n samples (high per-iteration cost) but does not need to store per-example gradients. SAG updates using one newly computed ∇ℓᵢ(w) and maintains a memory buffer of the latest gradient for each example, enabling an average gradient estimate with variance reduction at the cost of ≈ n×d storage.

questions.question_text: In the SMiLe / DAgger-style iterative imitation learning loop, which statement best describes the relationship between *trajectories* and the *training dataset* at iteration i?
options: A. Trajectories are continuously edited by replacing the agent’s actions with expert actions, and the edited trajectories are the dataset.
B. Trajectories are collected (and then kept fixed) to determine which states were visited; the dataset is a separate collection of (state, expert_action) labels for those visited states.
C. The dataset consists only of the final state of each trajectory paired with the expert’s action; intermediate states are ignored.
D. The dataset is identical to the trajectories because each trajectory already contains the expert action for every visited state.
questions.explanation: In these algorithms, you first roll out N trajectories under the current (or mixed) policy to discover which states the learner visits. Those trajectories are not modified after collection; they serve as a record of visited states. You then query the expert for the action at each visited state and store those labels in a separate supervised dataset of (state, expert_action) pairs used for training.

questions.question_text: In scikit-learn, what does `RandomForestRegressor.predict(X)` return by default?
options: A vector of class probabilities for each sample
A single continuous numeric prediction for each sample
A probability distribution over possible target values
A calibrated probability estimate of the target being above a threshold
questions.explanation: Regression models in scikit-learn (including `RandomForestRegressor`) output continuous predictions (e.g., a predicted CLV). They do not output class probabilities or a full probability distribution by default; probability outputs like `predict_proba` are associated with classifiers, not regressors.

questions.question_text:
A logistic regression model outputs probabilities \(\hat p(x)\). With the default decision rule \(\hat p(x) \ge 0.5 \Rightarrow \) predict positive, which situation most commonly leads to **high precision but lower recall**?

A. Many true positives have predicted probabilities just below 0.5, while only a few very confident cases exceed 0.5.
B. The model predicts positive for almost all samples, including most negatives.
C. The dataset is perfectly balanced and the classes are linearly separable.
D. The threshold is lowered from 0.5 to 0.2 without changing anything else.
options: A. Many true positives have predicted probabilities just below 0.5, while only a few very confident cases exceed 0.5.
B. The model predicts positive for almost all samples, including most negatives.
C. The dataset is perfectly balanced and the classes are linearly separable.
D. The threshold is lowered from 0.5 to 0.2 without changing anything else.
questions.explanation: With a fixed 0.5 cutoff, logistic regression can be conservative: it only predicts positive when \(\hat p(x)\) is clearly above 0.5. If many true positives sit at \(\hat p(x)\in[0.3,0.49]\), they become false negatives, reducing recall. Meanwhile, the few predicted positives (those above 0.5) are often correct, raising precision. Option B typically increases recall but hurts precision; option C would usually give both high precision and high recall; option D usually increases recall (and often reduces precision).

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the *training strategy* difference between Extra Trees and XGBoost?
options: Extra Trees builds trees sequentially to correct previous errors; XGBoost builds trees in parallel to reduce variance.
Extra Trees is a bagging-style ensemble that builds many randomized trees in parallel; XGBoost is a boosting method that builds trees sequentially to reduce error.
Extra Trees and XGBoost are both boosting methods; they mainly differ only in the loss function used.
Extra Trees chooses splits by optimizing a differentiable loss with gradients; XGBoost chooses splits fully at random.
questions.explanation: Extra Trees is a bagging-style method: it trains many trees in parallel and averages them (variance reduction). XGBoost is boosting: it adds trees sequentially, where each new tree focuses on correcting the mistakes of the current ensemble (bias reduction). The other options reverse or conflate these core ideas.

questions.question_text:
In permutation feature importance, what does shuffling a single feature column in the test set accomplish?

A. It changes the distribution of the feature values to match the training set
B. It preserves the feature’s distribution but breaks its relationship with the target, isolating its contribution to performance
C. It retrains the model without that feature to measure its marginal contribution
D. It increases the feature’s correlation with the target to test robustness
options: It changes the distribution of the feature values to match the training set
It preserves the feature’s distribution but breaks its relationship with the target, isolating its contribution to performance
It retrains the model without that feature to measure its marginal contribution
It increases the feature’s correlation with the target to test robustness
questions.explanation: Permutation importance works by randomly permuting (shuffling) one feature’s values in the evaluation data. This keeps the same set of values (so the marginal distribution is similar) but destroys the alignment between that feature and the target (and with other features), removing the useful signal that the model could exploit from that feature. The resulting performance drop estimates how much the model relied on that feature. It does not retrain the model (so C is incorrect), and it does not intentionally increase correlation (D).

questions.question_text: In Gaussian Process regression, what is the most accurate interpretation of using the inverse kernel matrix \(K(X,X)^{-1}\) when computing the predictive mean (e.g., \(\mu(x_*) = k_*^T K^{-1} y\))?
options: A. It amplifies the influence of training points that are highly correlated with many others, because they represent consensus information.
B. It decorrelates (removes redundancy among) training points by reweighting them so that unique points tend to have higher effective influence and redundant points are downweighted.
C. It makes all training points contribute equally by normalizing the kernel matrix so that each row sums to 1.
D. It replaces the kernel function with a linear kernel, making the model equivalent to linear regression.
questions.explanation: The kernel matrix K encodes similarity/correlation among training points. Multiplying by K^{-1} corresponds to using the precision matrix, which accounts for correlations and effectively decorrelates the training set. Redundant (highly correlated) points tend to be downweighted after accounting for the information already present in other points, while more unique points can have larger effective influence.

questions.question_text: In a noiseless Gaussian Process, the posterior mean at a test point \(\mathbf{x}_*\) can be written as \(\mu(\mathbf{x}_*) = \mathbf{k}_*^\top \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{y}\). Which statement best explains the role of \(\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{y}\) when training points are highly correlated (large off-diagonal entries in \(\mathbf{K}\))?
options: A. It increases the effective weights so that correlated points have more influence, since they confirm each other.
B. It leaves the weights unchanged because \(\mathbf{K}^{-1}\) is always the identity matrix for valid kernels.
C. It adjusts weights to reduce redundant influence, effectively “de-correlating” the contributions of similar training points.
D. It only affects the posterior variance, not the posterior mean, so correlation does not change the mean prediction.
questions.explanation: When training points are strongly correlated, their observations contain overlapping information. Multiplying by \(\mathbf{K}^{-1}\) counteracts this correlation structure: it subtracts off components of each observation that can be explained by the others, preventing double-counting. Thus \(\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{y}\) yields smaller (and sometimes even negative) effective weights compared to the uncorrelated case.

questions.question_text: In Gaussian Processes, when can the mean function m(x) be represented as a finite vector \(\mathbf{m}\)?
options: When the input domain \(\mathcal{X}\) is finite, e.g., \(\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}\), so \(\mathbf{m} = (m(x_1),\dots,m(x_n))^\top\).
Only when \(\mathcal{X}\) is continuous (e.g., \([0,1]\)), because then m(x) is a function and functions are vectors.
Only when the kernel is linear, because nonlinear kernels require an infinite-dimensional mean.
Whenever \(\mathcal{X}\) is countably infinite, because any countable set can be stored as a vector in memory.
questions.explanation: If \(\mathcal{X}\) contains a finite set of inputs \(\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}\), then the GP prior mean evaluated on that set is just a finite list of numbers, naturally written as a vector \(\mathbf{m}\in\mathbb{R}^n\) with entries \(m(x_i)=\mathbb{E}[f(x_i)]\). For a continuous domain, m(x) is a function (uncountably many points), not something you can store as a finite vector. Countably infinite domains yield an infinite sequence in principle, but not a finite vector you can literally store.

questions.question_text:
In Gaussian Process regression (assuming zero prior mean), the posterior mean at a test point \(x^*\) is computed as:

\[
 m_*(x^*) = K(x^*, X)\, K(X,X)^{-1}\, y
\]

Which interpretation best matches what this formula is doing?
options: A. It computes a weighted combination of the observed targets y, where weights depend on similarity between x* and training inputs and correlations among training inputs.
B. It computes the average of y and then scales it by the determinant of K(X,X) to regularize the prediction.
C. It ignores correlations among training points by using only the diagonal of K(X,X), ensuring independent contributions from each point.
D. It returns the kernel similarity K(x*, X) as the prediction, without using the observed targets y.
questions.explanation: The posterior mean is a linear combination of observed targets y. The vector K(x*, X) measures similarity between x* and each training input, while K(X,X)^{-1} accounts for correlations among training points (preventing double-counting redundant information). Options B, C, and D each remove essential parts of the GP update: B introduces unrelated determinant scaling, C discards off-diagonal correlations, and D omits the observed outputs y entirely.

questions.question_text: In exact Gaussian Process (GP) regression with \(n\) training points, what are the typical time and memory complexities associated with forming and inverting the kernel matrix \(K(X,X)\)?
options: A. Time \(O(n^2)\), Memory \(O(n)\)
B. Time \(O(n^3)\), Memory \(O(n^2)\)
C. Time \(O(n)\), Memory \(O(n^3)\)
D. Time \(O(n^2)\), Memory \(O(n^2\log n)\)
questions.explanation: For \(n\) points, \(K(X,X)\) is \(n\times n\), so storing it costs \(O(n^2)\) memory. Exact inversion/Cholesky-based solves scale cubically, typically \(O(n^3)\), which becomes impractical as \(n\) grows.

questions.question_text: In the toy Gaussian Process regression example (RBF kernel with variance 1 and length-scale 1, noiseless observations), which expression correctly gives the **predictive mean** at a test point \(x_*\)?
options: \(\mu(x_*) = \mathbf{k}_*(x_*)^\top\,\mathbf{K}^{-1}\,\mathbf{y}\)
\(\mu(x_*) = \mathbf{y}^\top\,\mathbf{K}\,\mathbf{k}_*(x_*)\)
questions.explanation: In standard GP regression (with zero mean prior and noiseless observations), the posterior predictive mean at a test input is a linear combination of observed targets: \(\mu(x_*) = \mathbf{k}_*^\top\mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{y}\). Option C is the predictive variance formula, and the other options have incorrect shapes/operations for producing a scalar mean.

questions.question_text: In the geometric interpretation of the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for binary classification, what does MCC measure between the true-label vector and the prediction vector?
options: A. The Euclidean distance between the two vectors
B. The cosine of the angle between the two vectors (a correlation-like alignment measure)
C. The ratio of true positives to predicted positives (precision)
D. The area under the ROC curve (AUC) computed from the two vectors
questions.explanation: Geometrically, MCC can be understood as a correlation-like measure: it reflects how well the prediction vector aligns with the true-label vector. This corresponds to the cosine of the angle between the vectors: +1 when perfectly aligned (0°), 0 when orthogonal (90°), and −1 when opposed (180°). The other options describe different metrics (distance, precision, AUC) that are not angle/cosine-based alignment measures.

questions.question_text: After fitting a BG/NBD model using `lifetimes.BetaGeoFitter`, which attribute returns the fitted model parameters as a Pandas Series (typically containing `r`, `alpha`, `a`, and `b`)?
options: A. `bgf.params_`
B. `bgf.parameters`
C. `bgf.summary_`
D. `bgf.coef_`
questions.explanation: `params_` is the standard lifetimes attribute that stores the fitted BG/NBD parameters as a Pandas Series (commonly `r`, `alpha`, `a`, `b`). The other options are plausible naming patterns from other libraries (e.g., `coef_`) or incorrect attribute names for `lifetimes`.

questions.question_text:
You fit a `GammaGammaFitter` and receive: **"Desired error not necessarily achieved due to precision loss"** followed by a `ConvergenceError`. Which interpretation is the most accurate?

A. The model successfully converged, but the warning is harmless and can be ignored.
B. The optimizer had numerical issues and could not reliably estimate parameters from the data.
C. The error occurs only when `frequency` contains zeros; otherwise it cannot happen.
D. The issue is caused by using the wrong Python version and is unrelated to the data.
options: A. The model successfully converged, but the warning is harmless and can be ignored.
B. The optimizer had numerical issues and could not reliably estimate parameters from the data.
C. The error occurs only when `frequency` contains zeros; otherwise it cannot happen.
D. The issue is caused by using the wrong Python version and is unrelated to the data.
questions.explanation: The message indicates the numerical optimizer struggled (precision loss) and therefore could not find a stable optimum; the resulting parameters are not reliably estimated. While zeros in `frequency` can be one cause (violating model requirements), convergence problems can also come from outliers, poor fit to Gamma-Gamma assumptions, too little data, or unsuitable optimization settings—so it is not exclusively due to zeros.

questions.question_text: In logistic regression, suppose the weight vector \(w\) is initialized to all zeros and the bias \(b\) is also initialized to 0. What is the predicted probability \(\hat{y}\) for any input \(x\) at initialization?
questions.explanation: With \(w=0\) and \(b=0\), the logit is \(z = w^T x + b = 0\). The sigmoid output is \(\sigma(0)=\frac{1}{1+e^0}=0.5\). This is why zero initialization produces a neutral 50% probability for every example (before any learning).

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes **grid search** for hyperparameter tuning?
options: A. It evaluates every combination in a predefined Cartesian product of hyperparameter values.
B. It samples hyperparameter combinations randomly from continuous distributions until convergence.
C. It updates hyperparameters using gradients computed on the validation loss.
D. It evaluates only one hyperparameter at a time while holding the others fixed.
questions.explanation: Grid search is defined by exhaustive evaluation over a predefined set of values for each hyperparameter, forming a Cartesian grid of combinations. Random sampling (B) describes random search. Gradient-based updates (C) are not grid/random search. One-at-a-time tuning (D) is closer to coordinate/line search and does not enumerate the full Cartesian product.

questions.question_text:
In group-level calibration, you compute:

```python
sum_actuals = df_group["SUBMITTED_APPLICATION"].sum()
sum_pred_probs = df_group["probability"].sum()
delta_sum = sum_actuals - sum_pred_probs
```

What must you do to convert `delta_sum` into a per-row probability adjustment that can be added to each row’s predicted probability in the group?
options: Add `delta_sum` directly to each row’s probability
Divide `delta_sum` by the group size (number of rows) to get `delta`
Multiply `delta_sum` by the group size to get `delta`
Apply a sigmoid to `delta_sum` to convert it to a probability
questions.explanation: `delta_sum` is in units of counts (expected number of positives minus actual positives), not a probability. To shift each row’s probability by a constant amount, you need a per-row quantity, so you normalize by group size: `delta = delta_sum / len(df_group)`. This makes the adjustment comparable to `actual_rate - pred_rate`.

questions.question_text:
In group-level probability calibration, you compute for each group:

```python
actual_rate = df_group["SUBMITTED_APPLICATION"].mean()
pred_rate   = df_group["probability"].mean()
delta       = actual_rate - pred_rate
new_prob    = np.clip(probability + delta, 0, 1)
```

What is the primary purpose of adding `delta` to each row’s probability within the group?
options: To force every individual prediction in the group to equal the group’s `actual_rate`
To shift the group’s average predicted probability so it matches the observed submission rate
To reduce model variance by shrinking probabilities toward 0.5
To improve ranking metrics (e.g., AUC) by reordering predictions within the group
questions.explanation: With additive calibration, `delta = actual_rate - pred_rate` is a constant offset applied to all examples in the group. This shifts the mean of the group’s predicted probabilities from `pred_rate` to approximately `actual_rate` (before clipping). It does not make every individual prediction equal to `actual_rate`, and it generally does not change ordering within the group (except for clipping effects), so it’s aimed at calibration rather than ranking.

questions.question_text:
You train an `ExtraTreesClassifier` on a dataset with 416 positive examples and 9,210 negative examples and evaluate using **accuracy**. The model achieves 95% accuracy but detects very few positives.

Which statement best explains what is happening and what metric is more appropriate to examine?
options: The model is likely biased toward predicting the majority class; examine recall/F1 (and/or Precision-Recall AUC) for the minority class.
The model is overfitting because ExtraTrees always overfits on tabular data; examine training loss to confirm.
The model is underfitting because the dataset is too small; examine R^2 to confirm.
The model is fine because 95% accuracy is strong; accuracy is the best metric for imbalanced classification.
questions.explanation: With severe class imbalance, a classifier can get high accuracy by predicting the majority class most of the time, while failing to identify the minority class. Metrics that focus on minority-class performance—especially recall, precision, F1-score, and often Precision-Recall AUC—reveal whether the model is actually finding positives.

questions.question_text:
In the BG/NBD model, which set of inputs is typically used for each customer to estimate future purchasing and the probability the customer is still active?

A. Frequency, recency, customer age (T)
B. Monetary value, discount rate, customer age (T)
C. Frequency, monetary value, discount rate
D. Recency, monetary value, acquisition channel
options: A. Frequency, recency, customer age (T)
B. Monetary value, discount rate, customer age (T)
C. Frequency, monetary value, discount rate
D. Recency, monetary value, acquisition channel
questions.explanation: BG/NBD uses transaction timing/count information: frequency (number of repeat purchases observed), recency (time since last purchase, often measured as time from first to last purchase), and customer age T (time observed since first purchase). Monetary value is not part of BG/NBD itself; it is typically modeled separately (e.g., with a Gamma-Gamma model) when computing CLV.

questions.question_text:
In the BG/NBD model, which pairing of latent customer parameters and prior distributions is used to model uncertainty in (1) purchase frequency and (2) dropout/churn?

Choose the option that correctly matches both components.
options: A. Purchase rate λ ~ Gamma; dropout probability p ~ Beta
B. Purchase rate λ ~ Beta; dropout probability p ~ Gamma
C. Purchase rate λ ~ Normal; dropout probability p ~ Bernoulli
D. Purchase rate λ ~ Uniform; dropout probability p ~ Poisson
questions.explanation: BG/NBD models heterogeneity (and thus uncertainty) by placing distributions over customer-level latent parameters. The transaction process uses a Poisson-like purchase rate λ with a Gamma mixing distribution, yielding a Negative Binomial form for repeat transactions across customers. Dropout is modeled as a geometric process with customer-specific probability p of becoming inactive after a transaction, and p is given a Beta distribution to represent uncertainty and variability across customers.

questions.question_text: In the standard train/validation split used purely for hyperparameter tuning, which statement is correct about how the validation set is used?
options: Validation loss is backpropagated to update model weights, but only after training on the training set is complete.
Validation data is used to update model weights during training to improve generalization.
Validation metrics are computed to choose hyperparameters, but gradients from the validation set do not update model weights.
Validation labels are used to compute an additional regularization term that is added to the training loss and backpropagated.
questions.explanation: In two-level optimization, the inner loop updates weights using only the training set. The validation set is used in the outer loop to evaluate a trained model and decide which hyperparameters to try next; no validation gradients flow into the weights.

questions.question_text:
In IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic (as used in typical ML training), which statement best explains why results may differ across runs even on a single machine?

Consider the expression:

(a + b) + c  vs  a + (b + c)
options: Floating-point addition is associative, so any run-to-run differences must come only from random seeds.
Floating-point addition is not associative due to rounding, so changing the order of operations can change the final result slightly.
IEEE 754 guarantees bit-for-bit identical results as long as the CPU model is the same.
Non-associativity only occurs in distributed training when gradients are reduced across multiple nodes.
questions.explanation: IEEE 754 floating-point numbers have finite precision, so each addition may round to the nearest representable value. Because rounding happens at each step, regrouping terms can change intermediate rounding and thus change the final result. This can occur within a single node when thread scheduling, vectorization, or library kernels change the effective summation order.

questions.question_text: In the context of machine learning data assumptions, what does **"identically distributed"** mean in **i.i.d.**?
options: A. Each sample is drawn from the same probability distribution, even if samples may be dependent
B. Each sample is drawn from the same probability distribution, and samples do not influence each other
C. Samples are drawn from different distributions, but the average distribution stays the same over time
D. Samples are drawn from the same dataset file, so they are automatically identically distributed
questions.explanation: I.I.D. means both **independent** and **identically distributed**. “Identically distributed” requires that every sample is generated from the **same underlying distribution** (e.g., same coin with P(Heads)=0.5 each time). Independence is a separate requirement: samples shouldn’t affect each other. Option B correctly includes both parts of i.i.d.; A omits independence, C contradicts “identical,” and D confuses “same file/source” with “same underlying distribution.”

questions.question_text:
In the provided RandomForestRegressor example, what do the values computed by `lower_bounds = np.percentile(tree_predictions, 2.5, axis=0)` and `upper_bounds = np.percentile(tree_predictions, 97.5, axis=0)` represent?

A. A 95% confidence interval for the mean prediction of the forest
B. A 95% prediction interval for each test instance based on the distribution of tree predictions
C. The 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles of the target variable `y_test`
D. The minimum and maximum predictions across trees for each test instance
options: A. A 95% confidence interval for the mean prediction of the forest
B. A 95% prediction interval for each test instance based on the distribution of tree predictions
C. The 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles of the target variable `y_test`
D. The minimum and maximum predictions across trees for each test instance
questions.explanation: The code collects predictions from each individual tree and then takes the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles across trees for each sample (axis=0). This yields an empirical central 95% interval of the ensemble members’ predictions per test instance, commonly used as a simple prediction-interval heuristic. It is not a confidence interval for the mean (A), does not use `y_test` (C), and percentiles are not the same as min/max (D).

questions.question_text:
In classical ordinary least squares (OLS) linear regression, which approach is typically used to construct a 95% confidence interval for the *mean prediction* at a new input point?

A. Use the model's standard error of prediction and the t-distribution
B. Use bootstrapping to resample the training set many times and take the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles
C. Use the variance across trees in a random forest ensemble
D. Use the posterior predictive distribution from a Bayesian model
options: Use the model's standard error of prediction and the t-distribution
Use bootstrapping to resample the training set many times and take the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles
Use the variance across trees in a random forest ensemble
Use the posterior predictive distribution from a Bayesian model
questions.explanation: In OLS linear regression, under standard assumptions, the sampling distribution of the estimated mean response at a new point can be expressed using a standard error derived from the fitted model; a t-distribution is then used to form a confidence interval (especially when the noise variance is estimated). Bootstrapping, ensemble variance, and Bayesian posterior intervals are valid uncertainty approaches in other settings, but they are not the classical, direct OLS method.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes how **Shannon entropy** relates to the information content of a sentence (as computed from word probabilities within the sentence)?
options: A. Entropy is higher when the sentence contains a few words repeated many times, because repetition increases unpredictability.
B. Entropy is higher when the word distribution is more uniform (less predictable), indicating higher information content.
C. Entropy is always maximized by longer sentences, regardless of the word distribution.
D. Entropy measures grammatical complexity (e.g., number of clauses), not word unpredictability.
questions.explanation: Shannon entropy measures uncertainty in a probability distribution. When a sentence’s word probabilities are more uniform (many different words with similar frequencies), the next token is less predictable, so entropy increases. Heavy repetition concentrates probability mass on a few words, making the distribution more predictable and lowering entropy. Length alone does not guarantee higher entropy, and entropy is not a direct grammar/complexity metric.

questions.question_text: In the BG/NBD model input summary for each customer, which definition of **frequency** is correct?
options: A. The total number of transactions including the first purchase
B. The number of repeat transactions (purchases after the first one)
C. The time between the first and the most recent transaction
D. The total time from the first purchase to the end of the observation period
questions.explanation: In BG/NBD, frequency is defined as the number of *repeat* purchases, i.e., transactions after the first one. Recency is the time between first and most recent purchase, and T is the customer 'age' from first purchase to the end of the observation window.

questions.question_text: Which undersampling method explicitly aims to keep majority-class points that are *closest* to minority-class points, so that the retained majority samples are most informative near the decision boundary?
options: A. Cluster-Based Undersampling (e.g., KMeans + sample per cluster)
B. NearMiss undersampling
C. Random undersampling without constraints
D. Feature-importance diversity sampling
questions.explanation: NearMiss selects majority-class samples based on proximity to minority-class samples. By keeping majority points that are closest to minority points, it concentrates retained examples near the likely classification boundary, which tends to be more informative for learning separation between classes. Cluster-based undersampling focuses on coverage/diversity of the majority distribution rather than boundary proximity.

questions.question_text:
In linear regression with mean squared error, you compute the gradient with respect to weights as:

\(\mathbf{dw} = \frac{1}{m}\mathbf{X}^\top(\hat{\mathbf{y}}-\mathbf{y})\).

Which statement best describes what \(\mathbf{X}^\top(\hat{\mathbf{y}}-\mathbf{y})\) is doing?
options: A. It scales every element of X by the same constant so all weights change equally.
B. It computes, for each feature, the sum over samples of (error × that feature value), producing one gradient component per weight.
C. It computes the prediction vector \(\hat{\mathbf{y}}\) from \(\mathbf{X}\) and \(\mathbf{w}\).
D. It averages the errors across samples to produce a single scalar gradient shared by all weights.
questions.explanation: \(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{error}\) produces an n-dimensional vector where component j equals \(\sum_i \text{error}_i\,x_{ij}\). This aggregates how feature j aligns with the residuals across all samples, giving a separate update signal for each weight. It is not uniform scaling (A), not computing predictions (C), and not collapsing to a single scalar (D).

questions.question_text: Which statement best captures the *intuition* behind the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)?
options: MCC is essentially the Pearson correlation between predicted labels and true labels, giving +1 for perfect agreement, 0 for random, and −1 for perfect disagreement.
MCC is equivalent to accuracy, but rescaled to the range [−1, +1].
MCC measures only how good the model is at predicting the positive class (like precision/recall), ignoring true negatives.
MCC is the geometric mean of precision and recall, adjusted for class imbalance.
questions.explanation: MCC is designed to behave like a correlation (analogous to Pearson correlation) between predictions and ground truth: +1 when they align perfectly, −1 when they are perfectly opposite, and 0 when there is no relationship (random guessing). It explicitly accounts for all four confusion-matrix terms (TP, TN, FP, FN), unlike accuracy-only or positive-class-only metrics.

questions.question_text:
In machine learning evaluation, which situation most directly calls for the harmonic mean instead of the arithmetic mean?

A. Averaging model accuracies across 5 folds (each fold counted equally)
B. Averaging inference times per sample when each benchmark run uses the same number of samples
C. Averaging speeds (or throughputs) measured over segments that all have the same distance (or same amount of work)
D. Averaging training losses from checkpoints taken at equal time intervals
options: A. Averaging model accuracies across 5 folds (each fold counted equally)
B. Averaging inference times per sample when each benchmark run uses the same number of samples
C. Averaging speeds (or throughputs) measured over segments that all have the same distance (or same amount of work)
D. Averaging training losses from checkpoints taken at equal time intervals
questions.explanation: The harmonic mean is appropriate when averaging rates/ratios over a fixed total amount of “work” (e.g., same distance, same number of items). In that case, slower rates contribute more time and should pull the average down more, which is exactly what the harmonic mean captures. Options A and D are simple equal-weight averages of values, so arithmetic mean fits. Option B describes equal sample counts; if you already have per-sample times and each run has the same number of samples, a simple arithmetic mean of per-sample times is appropriate.

questions.question_text:
In scikit-learn's IsolationForest, what does the output of `fit_predict()` represent?

Consider the code:
```python
iso_forest = IsolationForest(contamination=0.05, random_state=42)
labels = iso_forest.fit_predict(X)
```
What do the values in `labels` mean?
options: A. Values are continuous anomaly scores where lower means more anomalous
B. `-1` indicates an outlier and `1` indicates a normal (inlier) point
C. `0` indicates an outlier and `1` indicates a normal (inlier) point
D. `1` indicates an outlier and `-1` indicates a normal (inlier) point
questions.explanation: In scikit-learn, `IsolationForest.fit_predict()` returns discrete labels: `-1` for predicted outliers and `1` for predicted inliers. Continuous-valued anomaly scores come from methods like `decision_function()`, not `fit_predict()`.

questions.question_text: In K-Means clustering, what does **inertia** measure?
options: A. The sum of squared distances from each point to the centroid of its assigned cluster
B. The average (non-squared) distance from each point to the nearest centroid across all clusters
C. The number of points that changed cluster assignment in the last iteration
D. The distance between the two farthest centroids
questions.explanation: Inertia in K-Means is defined as the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS): for each data point, compute the squared Euclidean distance to the centroid of the cluster it is assigned to, then sum across all points. The other options describe different quantities (e.g., assignment changes, inter-centroid distances) that are not inertia.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why standard K-Means does **not** inherently enforce balanced cluster sizes?
options: A. K-Means minimizes within-cluster variance (sum of squared distances), not the difference in the number of points per cluster.
B. K-Means always assigns exactly n/k points to each cluster unless there are duplicate points.
C. K-Means optimizes a density objective that forces clusters to have equal mass.
D. K-Means uses medoids rather than centroids, which prevents balancing cluster sizes.
questions.explanation: Standard K-Means optimizes an objective like the within-cluster sum of squared distances (inertia). Nothing in that objective penalizes unequal cluster cardinalities, so it may produce highly imbalanced cluster sizes if that reduces within-cluster variance. Options B and C describe properties K-Means does not have, and D confuses K-Means with K-Medoids (which still does not automatically enforce balance unless constrained).

questions.question_text:
In a Kaplan–Meier survival analysis for subscription churn, which pair of definitions is most appropriate?

A. Duration = number of transactions; Event = whether a transaction occurred (1/0)
B. Duration = time from subscription start to churn or censoring; Event = whether churn occurred (1/0)
C. Duration = time between transactions; Event = whether a second transaction occurred (1/0)
D. Duration = total revenue; Event = whether revenue exceeded a threshold (1/0)
options: A. Duration = number of transactions; Event = whether a transaction occurred (1/0)
B. Duration = time from subscription start to churn or censoring; Event = whether churn occurred (1/0)
C. Duration = time between transactions; Event = whether a second transaction occurred (1/0)
D. Duration = total revenue; Event = whether revenue exceeded a threshold (1/0)
questions.explanation: Kaplan–Meier models time-to-event. For churn analysis, the time axis should be a meaningful elapsed time (e.g., days/months since subscription start) and the event indicator should mark whether the churn event happened (1) or the observation was censored (0, e.g., still active at data cutoff). The other options confuse time-to-event with transaction counts, inter-transaction timing without defining churn, or non-time outcomes like revenue.

questions.question_text:
In the Kaplan-Meier example, what does the `churn` column represent when passed to `event_observed=df['churn']`?

```python
kmf.fit(durations=df['duration'], event_observed=df['churn'])
```
options: A. The probability that a user will churn at each time step
B. The event indicator: 1 if churn occurred (event observed), 0 if the observation is right-censored (still active)
C. The total number of users who churned by the end of the study
D. The time since the user last logged in
questions.explanation: In Kaplan-Meier survival analysis, `event_observed` is a binary indicator of whether the event occurred. Here, `churn=1` means the churn event was observed at `duration`, while `churn=0` means the user had not churned by the measurement time, so the `duration` is right-censored (we only know they survived at least that long).

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why the Kaplan–Meier estimator is called **non-parametric**?
options: It has no inputs other than time and therefore has zero parameters.
It does not assume a specific underlying probability distribution (e.g., exponential or Weibull) for survival times.
It estimates a single parameter (like λ) from an assumed exponential survival distribution.
It requires tuning hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate) to fit the survival curve.
questions.explanation: Kaplan–Meier is non-parametric because it avoids assuming a parametric form (exponential/Weibull/normal, etc.) for the survival-time distribution. Instead, it constructs the survival curve directly from observed event times and censoring information. It still relies on inputs (time-to-event and censoring indicators), but not on distributional parameters like λ.

questions.question_text: In a Kaplan–Meier survival analysis for customer churn, what is the most typical **starting point** (time origin) used to compute each customer’s duration?
options: The customer’s last transaction date
The customer’s first transaction (or subscription start) date
The date the model is trained
A randomly chosen date to align all customers
questions.explanation: Kaplan–Meier requires a consistent time origin for durations. In churn/customer survival, the standard baseline is the first transaction (or subscription start), and time-to-event (or censoring time) is measured forward from that point.

questions.question_text: In Matplotlib line plots where the x-axis represents binned categories (e.g., quartiles), how do you place x-axis labels so they appear centered in each bin rather than on the bin boundary lines?
options: A. Set xticks at the bin edges and rotate labels by 45 degrees
B. Set xticks at the midpoints of adjacent bin edges and use the bin labels
C. Use plt.legend() with quartile labels so labels attach to bins automatically
D. Use plt.grid(True) so gridlines align labels to the center of bins
questions.explanation: To label bins (intervals) instead of boundary lines, you must place tick positions at the center of each interval. Computing midpoints as (edge_i + edge_{i+1})/2 and then calling plt.xticks(midpoints, labels) centers the labels within each bin. Rotating labels, using legends, or enabling grids does not change tick placement.

questions.question_text:
In the provided approach, why is `survival_df.reset_index()` used before calling `pd.merge_asof`?

```python
survival_df_reset = survival_df.reset_index().rename(columns={'index': 'time'})
```
options: A. To convert the survival probabilities into cumulative hazards required by `merge_asof`
B. To make the time index a regular column so it can be used as the right-side merge key
C. To remove duplicate time points because `merge_asof` cannot handle duplicates
D. To sort the survival curve by time automatically without calling `sort_values`
questions.explanation: `merge_asof` merges using columns (via `left_on`/`right_on`), not an index directly. Kaplan–Meier outputs often store time in the index, so resetting the index exposes time as a column (renamed to `time`) that can be referenced in `right_on='time'`. It does not automatically sort (D), does not convert to hazards (A), and does not inherently remove duplicates (C).

questions.question_text:
In a logistic regression model

$$\log\Big(\tfrac{P(y=1)}{P(y=0)}\Big)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x_1+\cdots$$

what does it mean if \(\beta_1\) is negative?
options: A. Increasing \(x_1\) increases the odds of \(y=1\) (and increases the predicted probability), holding other variables constant.
B. Increasing \(x_1\) decreases the log-odds of \(y=1\) (and typically decreases the predicted probability), holding other variables constant.
C. A negative \(\beta_1\) implies \(x_1\) is unimportant and should always be removed from the model.
D. A negative \(\beta_1\) means the model is invalid because logistic regression coefficients must be nonnegative.
questions.explanation: In logistic regression, coefficients are additive effects on the log-odds. If \(\beta_1 < 0\), then increasing \(x_1\) reduces the log-odds of the positive class (and therefore reduces the odds \(P(y=1)/P(y=0)\)). Because the logistic function is monotonic, this typically corresponds to a lower predicted probability of \(y=1\), all else equal.

questions.question_text: Why is log-loss (cross-entropy) typically reported as an average over instances (i.e., divided by N) rather than as a raw sum over the dataset?
options: A. Averaging makes the metric comparable across datasets/folds of different sizes and interpretable as per-example loss
B. Averaging makes the loss convex; summing would make it non-convex
C. Averaging is required to ensure probabilities p_i stay between 0 and 1
D. Averaging prevents overfitting by shrinking the loss toward zero
questions.explanation: The summed negative log-likelihood grows roughly linearly with the number of samples, so its magnitude depends on dataset size. Dividing by N yields a per-instance quantity that is interpretable (average “surprise” per example) and comparable across datasets, folds, and batch sizes. Convexity does not depend on averaging vs summing (both are convex for logistic loss), probability constraints are unrelated, and averaging does not directly prevent overfitting.

questions.question_text:
In a multi-class classification setting, which statement best describes the **macro average** of a metric such as F1-score?

A. Compute global TP/FP/FN across all classes, then compute one F1 from those totals
B. Compute the metric per class, then take the unweighted mean across classes
C. Compute the metric per class, then average weighted by the number of predicted samples per class
D. Compute the metric per class, then average weighted by the number of true samples (support) per class
options: A. Compute global TP/FP/FN across all classes, then compute one F1 from those totals
B. Compute the metric per class, then take the unweighted mean across classes
C. Compute the metric per class, then average weighted by the number of predicted samples per class
D. Compute the metric per class, then average weighted by the number of true samples (support) per class
questions.explanation: Macro averaging treats each class equally: you compute the metric independently for each class (one-vs-rest) and then take the simple (unweighted) average across classes. Option A describes micro averaging. Option D describes weighted averaging using true class support. Option C is not the standard definition for macro/micro/weighted as commonly used in ML evaluation.

questions.question_text: In pysubgroup-style bucket notation, what does the interval expression `channel_name_bucket: [2:3[` mean?
options: A. It selects rows where `2 ≤ channel_name_bucket < 3`
B. It selects rows where `2 ≤ channel_name_bucket ≤ 3`
C. It selects rows where `2 < channel_name_bucket < 3`
D. It performs Python slicing and selects the 2nd and 3rd elements
questions.explanation: `[2:3[` is a mathematical half-open interval: it includes 2 and excludes 3, i.e., `2 ≤ x < 3`. It is not Python slicing, and it is not inclusive of the right endpoint.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) is considered a single, global metric for binary classification?
options: MCC is computed only from precision and recall, which are global by definition.
MCC combines TP, TN, FP, and FN into one correlation-like value over the entire dataset.
MCC is identical to accuracy, so it cannot be decomposed by class.
MCC can be computed per class, but most libraries choose not to report it.
questions.explanation: MCC explicitly uses all four confusion-matrix cells (TP, TN, FP, FN) in a single formula, producing one numerator and one denominator that summarize agreement between predictions and true labels across the whole dataset. This makes it intrinsically a global measure rather than something naturally defined “per class” like precision/recall.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) for binary classification?
options: A. MCC is equivalent to accuracy and only uses TP and TN.
B. MCC summarizes a confusion matrix using TP, TN, FP, and FN, and is especially informative with imbalanced classes.
C. MCC is the same as precision and ignores TN and FN.
D. MCC can only take values between 0 and 1, where 1 is perfect.
questions.explanation: MCC incorporates all four confusion-matrix terms (TP, TN, FP, FN), making it a balanced metric even when classes are imbalanced. Accuracy/precision do not use all four terms, and MCC ranges from −1 to +1 (not just 0 to 1).

questions.question_text: Which metric is *threshold-independent* and primarily measures how well a classifier separates classes across all possible decision thresholds?
options: Accuracy
ROC AUC
F1 score
Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)
questions.explanation: ROC AUC evaluates the model’s ranking/separation ability by considering performance across all classification thresholds. Accuracy, F1, and MCC are computed after choosing a specific threshold (implicitly or explicitly), so they are threshold-dependent.

questions.question_text: Which library performs Bayesian optimization using the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) algorithm as its primary Bayesian approach?
options: A. scikit-optimize (skopt)
B. Hyperopt
C. BayesianOptimization (bayes-opt)
D. Ax (with BoTorch)
questions.explanation: Hyperopt’s Bayesian optimization approach is commonly implemented via the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE). In contrast, bayes-opt is typically Gaussian Process-based, skopt provides Bayesian optimization integrated with scikit-learn (often GP-based), and Ax is an experimentation platform that can use BoTorch for advanced Bayesian optimization methods.

questions.question_text:
In multi-class classification with XGBoost, what does `model.predict(dtest)` typically return, and how do you convert it into a single predicted class label per sample?

```python
proba = model.predict(dtest)
y_pred = np.argmax(proba, axis=1)
```
options: A. `model.predict(dtest)` returns class labels directly; `np.argmax` is unnecessary
B. `model.predict(dtest)` returns probabilities per class; `np.argmax(..., axis=1)` selects the most likely class index for each sample
C. `model.predict(dtest)` returns feature importances; `np.argmax` selects the most important feature per sample
D. `model.predict(dtest)` returns raw margins; `np.argmax(..., axis=0)` selects the best class per sample
questions.explanation: For multi-class objectives, XGBoost’s `predict` commonly outputs an array of per-class probabilities for each sample (shape roughly `(n_samples, n_classes)`). To obtain a discrete class prediction per sample, you take the index of the maximum probability along the class dimension, which is `axis=1`.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes why multicollinearity is typically **more problematic** in linear/logistic regression than in tree-based models?
options: A. Linear/logistic regression estimates coefficients for all features simultaneously, so correlated predictors can make coefficient estimates unstable and hard to interpret.
B. Tree-based models cannot split on correlated features, so they fail to train when multicollinearity is present.
C. Multicollinearity always reduces predictive accuracy in any model, but linear models are the only ones that report it.
D. Linear/logistic regression automatically removes redundant features, while tree-based models keep all redundant features and overfit.
questions.explanation: Linear and logistic regression fit a single set of coefficients jointly across all predictors. When predictors are highly correlated, many coefficient combinations explain the data similarly, which inflates uncertainty (large standard errors) and makes coefficients sensitive to small data changes—hurting interpretability. Tree-based models choose split features one at a time and don’t rely on simultaneous coefficient estimation, so multicollinearity is usually less harmful for predictive performance.

questions.question_text:
In pandas, what happens when you call `df.corr()` on a DataFrame that includes boolean columns (True/False) along with numeric columns?

A. Boolean columns are ignored because Pearson correlation only supports floats.
B. Boolean columns are automatically converted to 1/0 and included in the Pearson correlation.
C. `df.corr()` raises a TypeError unless you manually cast booleans to integers.
D. Boolean columns are treated as strings ("True"/"False"), so correlations become NaN.
